text: I spent weeks building a cyberpunk PvP arena just so I could watch my friends fight each other inside of it. But it's not enough for me to simply take pleasure in watching my friends beat each other up. First, I wanted to watch them battle their own demons. >> That's a really big drop. >> So, I put my full Minecraft building skills to work, creating a twisted maze of buildings and secret pathways just so they'd struggle to even find each other. This elevator shaft here has a ladder clutch at the bottom. And this other part of the elevator shaft has hidden slime blocks. You can't trust a single painting you see on this map. And there are hidden doors pretty much everywhere you look. And I haven't even gotten into all the parkour challenges yet. If you're brave enough to jump all the way up to the top of this crane, you can even find a mace. Oh, but did I mention we've only looked at half the level? That's right. I built an entire underside of the map, complete with an abandoned subway station, secret squatter camps, and giant storage vats with hidden loot inside. It's safe to say my friends thought I was pretty unhinged. >> I don't want to be here anymore. How do I get down? Mog Mog, how do I get down from here? >> Look at this train. This is nuts. Now, you might think that everyone loses when participating in my madness, but believe it or not, there actually was a winner.

What is up party people? My YouTube channel is called Mog Swamp. I make gaming content primarily focused on Minecraft and specifically people call me the king of Superflat. Vertical live streaming has been incredible for my channel. It's that direct interaction viewers can get with their favorite content creator. That is what makes live stream so engaging and so fun to be a part of. You guys are ridiculous. The thing I love most about vertical live streams is just being able to meet people where they are in the short speed. There's so much discoverability. The number of people who watch my live streams has traditionally been very small. But since I started vertical live streaming, I've seen that percentage grow and grow and grow. You're one of my favorite YouTubers. Thank you, Leo. That means a lot to me, dude. Whereas with the vertical stream, I feel a lot more comfortable just being myself and sort of just talking with my chat. 10 push-ups. We just did the situps. My approach to my community is that everybody needs like a little break from life sometimes, and so we try to just create a little bit of an escapist bubble for us all to just hang out in. I love hanging out with you guys. It feels like people are a little bit more willing to engage in the fan funding in the vertical streams. Probably just cuz it feels so much more immediate, like we're having a back and forth conversation. I'll try to answer whatever questions you may have. Gifts are a form of fan funding that you can use in vertical live streams. They're fun, they're accessible, and there's just no pressure. You're just sending your favorite creator a gift at the end of the day. Kelsey K, thank you for the trophy cake for 1,000 jewels. When people send gifts, it's really cool to actually say what the thing they sent is. You want to try to validate that choice that they made. Chelsea Cheetah, thank you for the laughing disco ball. The entire chat gets lit up with this really, really cool animation and it really just hypes everybody up and makes it feel like a positive place to be. Doggy Tamer with the fireworks. The gift combos are really, really exciting. There's just something about the multiplier that makes you feel like, "Oh my god, we're going crazy." Athena, thank you for the flower combo. It's like a whole bouquet. I got five flowers. The main benefit I've seen on my channel is just a lot more people who didn't used to be able to give fan funding being able to get involved. Since there's all different bundles of jewels you can buy, it's very, very accessible for different viewers. So much of my channel is about positivity and just having a really inclusive and chill vibe. And I think gifts powered by jewels really aligns with my channel ethos in that way. You guys are the best. Thank you so much for all the support. If you haven't tried vertical live streams, I would definitely give it a shot. I had a lot of fun today. I love you guys. I'll see you on the next one. Bye.

These liinal paintings kind of feel like they might be holding a dark secret, but I had no idea where they would actually end up leading me. They bring me back to those early days of Minecraft when despite the deep loneliness and almost eerie feeling of the empty world, there were these small moments of beauty and such an endless curiosity for what mysteries the environment might hold. The artist is named Joji, and I was immediately drawn to the recurring themes of super flat worlds and strange village generation present in his work. This kind of thing has been my daily backdrop while playing in my super flat survival world over the last 13 years, and it's come to bring me a strange sort of comfort. This one really reminds me of sleeping underneath all of my terraforming projects. I reached out to him, hoping to support his work by commissioning a piece of my super flat world. So, I sent him my world file and soon he began sending back sketches of various spots in my world. But something just clicked when he took inspiration from this Monae painting to create this sketch of my main tower. After I gave it the thumbs up, it took a little while to see the finished product, but the result was so worth it. I just knew I had to get it printed on canvas. I was so excited when I picked it up because I had the perfect spot in my living room to hang in. Waking up and walking out of my bedroom to see this incredible work of art is just the best reminder of how far this little Minecraft world has taken

I made a giant lava cast mountain and now I'm painstakingly hollowing out the entire thing. But I'm not just throwing away countless hours because I hate myself. The issue is that I made the lava cast all the way back in November 2012. I never could have anticipated that I'd still be playing in my super flat world or all the problems the mountain would cause. By making mountains in this way, you end up with hundreds of small dark holes inside the mountain. Each one of these holes is constantly checking if it can spawn mobs, meaning the mountain is absolutely packed with bats. And some of the holes are even tall enough for other mobs to spawn inside. and it kills the rates of any mob farms I build nearby. After over a decade of building in the area, the frame rate at my base is pretty bad. So, my only choice is to hollow this thing out. And the worst part is cobblestone can't be instin. And I have a rule in my world against TNT duping. So, we're blasting this out the oldfashioned way with hard work. If you don't believe me, I made a video on my side channel, Mor Swamp, to prove

Recently, Minecraft has had a huge controversy surrounding the creeper with the whole internet debating if the creeper's exploding mechanic is fun or not and whether or not it would be added by Mojang today, but honestly, I don't really mind creepers at all. It's pretty rare that a creeper gets the best of me these days, and usually it's just because I've AFKed somewhere I really shouldn't have. But Enderman, well, that's a different story. These griefers destroy my terraforming all the time, and it got so bad, I had to download a special data pack to prevent them from picking up blocks. And I'm not alone. Popular servers like Hermitcraft have done the exact same thing. Mojang says they realized during the Enderman's development that just randomly picking up blocks would end up grieving your build. So, they changed it so they could only pick up a list of natural blocks, but players still build with these blocks all the time. And to make matters worse, they keep on adding new blocks to the list. The plants from the Nether update, rooted dirt, mud, torch flowers, pale moss and eye blossoms, and even cactus flowers. And did you know they can pick up TNT blocks? Bro wants to be a creeper so bad. But I have an easy fix for this horribly annoying mechanic. Just make it so Enderman can only pick up blocks that were not placed by a player. Things that grow or generate naturally are fair game. But if I place a block somewhere, it's probably cuz I wanted to stay there. Now, who's with me?

My super flat world recently turned 13 years old, and I realized that I hadn't actually done a world tour or a free world download in almost 4 years. So, I wanted to see what actually changed in my world in those 4 years. And I did not expect there to be such a huge difference. Massive mega structures that I've gotten so used to seeing just weren't there back then. And so much detail is missing from areas like the harbor that have really come to life since the last tour in 2021. My Nether dimension had just been reset at the time, so I had a dinky little temporary base instead of the awesome castle I have now. and my end dimension was basically empty instead of being the bustling cyberpunk city it is today. And a lot more than just my world has transformed. When I posted the last world tour, I had 5,630 subscribers and now we're at 2.6 million. But to be fair, some things haven't changed at all. Like my creepy wall of fireballs or my petrified oaks lab collection. If you want to explore my world for yourself, the free world download is linked in the description of the world tour on my side channel, More Swamp.

These might be the most insane Minecraft builds I've ever seen. They come from a channel called Brick Farmer 10. She makes these Minecraft ships that actually function using the Create mod, which adds technology like gears, conveyor belts, and trains into the game. Now, I don't know much about Create Mod since I've never tried it, but this channel is probably the best advertisement the mod could possibly have. This ship is called the Rust Roller, inspired by the artwork of Scottish concept artist Ian McHugh. It features a giant rotating wheel textured to perfection, a working elevator to board the craft, and even a cannon turret to defend the ship from attacks. Next up is the Copper Fly, a flying submarine with four massive propellers. The ship is loaded with cannons and even features extending gangways for docking. Then there's the Pale Wing, an alien looking spacecraft with a distinctive ring in the center. The ship features all the usual functionality, but even more impressive is how Brick Farmer managed to fit in a coherent interior complete with bunks for the crew. And finally, we have the Host, a cargo ship with an absolutely crazy looking takeoff. The ship can pick up a massive bomb using magnets and then deliver the payload with devastating consequences for whoever is underneath, which I assume is what will happen to you if you don't go subscribe to Brick Farmer

There's no way you can even name all the different ways to use dye in Minecraft because there's just so many and they keep adding new ones. And yet all the time I see people demanding dable wood or dable item frames or saddles or even villagers. But I've never seen someone suggest dable water until I came across this video by a super underrated creator, Josilia. She has a major gripe with the way that most of the water in the game is a deep blue, which is something I've never really thought about. So, she ended up finding a mod called Colored Water by Koari, and it works just as you'd expect, allowing you to dye water, place it wherever, and even put it in cauldrons. It also has condensed water made with extra dye that makes the liquid more opaque, and luminous water that glows when you add in glowstone dust. In her video, Josie made a crazy Ender Oasis using the purple water, a scenic sugary candy land with the pink water, and an enhanced jungle biome using the green water. I was so impressed with all her builds that I was beginning to be convinced on the whole dable water thing. So, I decided to try it out myself. And first, I made a sewer oozing radioactive sludge. I used the light blue water to create a prismatic spring. And I combined campfires and black condensed water to make a tar pit with a woolly mammoth. I was skeptical at first, but maybe we need dable water in

You are a dummy. There, I said it. You guys in the comments think you understand what block vomit is, but you all have no idea what you're talking about. So, it's time for a little lesson from someone with an actual degree in art. Okay, simply using blocks for things other than their intended purpose is not block vomit. That's just called creativity. You people also scream at Lego for using little pink frogs as the leaves in their cherry tree. Good builds have texture, but the best textures actually follow a pattern. Like the pearl and noise pattern I use to make asphalt in my world. This is not block vomit. Or even this Twitter build, not block vomit. It may look a bit crazy up close in survival, but they've carefully chosen each color to add to the overall composition, much like a pointless painter. Some builds are only meant to be viewed from a distance, and that's okay. But the way I used to build in my world, just throwing a bunch of random blocks in wherever with no rhyme or reason. Yeah, this I'll admit this is block vomit. It looks like TV static, and it hurts my brain. So, next time someone yells block vomit in the comments of some talented builder who dares to use a dried kelp block as something other than literal kelp, send them this video so I can tell

I absolutely hate when people use this one build trick. When you place a coral block outside of water in Minecraft, it eventually dries out, giving you this super unique grayscale texture. For years now, builders have used this as a substitute for stone in order to add some texture and variety to old stone walls and paths. But I don't hate it because I think it looks bad. I hate it because I'm jealous. It's easy spamming these all over your builds when you have unlimited coral reefs to destroy. But if you play in super flat survival like I do, it creates quite the conundrum. You see, since my world is in a plains biome and coral blocks can't be farmed or duplicated in any way, the only way I can get coral is by buying it from the watering trader whenever he decides to sell it. I don't even want to know where he gets it from. Anyways, this means my coral supply is extremely limited. So, every time I want to use it, I have a tough decision to make. I can use the coral's bright and uniquely colorful textures to make coral reefs in my harbor, old trims for my fancy buildings, and even beautiful flowers for my gardens. Or I can let the coral dry out just so that I can get a little bit of variation and detail into my stone walls and paths. What would you use it for?

Build stone is a new movement sweeping the Minecraft building scene which combines redstone with building to add movement to builds and bring them to life. And recently, this really cool technique for creating bubbles and waterfalls was shared by MckelC 1397. By putting an armor stand inside of a bubble column, you can get these really cool water particles. I wanted to try it out, but I realized I don't really have any waterfalls in my world to use the technique on. But I did recently build this beautiful fountain as part of the insane project I'm working on in the related video. So, I decided to try it out on these skinny diagonal water streams I made out of glass panes. I put the magma block in place and then dispensed an armor stand. one block below that on top of a carpet. Sure enough, the bubbles started spawning, but you could see the magma block on the outside of the fountain. I fixed it by replacing the slabs with stairs, and you would never even know the difference. The final step was to cover over the magma block with a map of a solid color. In my super flat world, I have quite a few easy options. I can go into the Nether for gray or a grassy area for green. Plus, I have old maps of my world with water for blue. I decided that gray would probably blend in the best, and I actually didn't even have to go to the Nether since I've dug out so much of my world down to bedrock. Oh, yeah, gray was a good call. Dude, you can barely see it. What other build stone techniques should I try out in my [Applause]

I never thought I'd say this, but my latest Minecraft video got me on the news. Mog Swamp, a channel with more than two and a half million subscribers. This is pretty impressive stuff. Oh, yeah. You know, Ernie, this is about taking Minecraft and pushing it to the limit. It all started when I visited San Antonio for the first time after moving to Austin in 2020. I saw this skyscraper downtown that really captured my attention with its pointy green roof and gothic details. I wanted to find out more, so I ended up poking around the tower, but most of it was close off to the public. I decided to try recreating the tower in Minecraft, and that's when my interest turned into an obsession. As I poured through blueprints and historical photos, it turns out San Antonio had been plagued with terrible flooding until they did some massive terraforming projects to redirect the river running through downtown, which turned this awkward peninsula into the perfect site for development. It took me hundreds of hours to design the build, clear out all the dirt, build all of the farms necessary to get the resources, and finally build the tower. But all of my time was somehow worth it because people actually took notice. The video quickly started picking up views and the next thing I knew I was driving to San Antonio for an interview. I had no idea what to expect, but the news people were actually super nice and chill. I'm always trying to one up myself and go bigger and bigger and basically just share my creativity with people. Deon Karp, KAT2 News.

Over the years, more and more mobs, blocks, and items have been added to Minecraft to the point where there are now over 1,600 unique textures that need to be done by anyone hoping to create their own texture pack. But now, imagine having to do all of that by hand. It might seem impossible, but that's exactly what these creators have set out to do. First is Creative Patty, who has been painstakingly drawing all of the textures in the game with a blue ballpoint pen. Their texture pack gives the game such a unique feeling, like my doodles and math class sprang to life and became their own world. And when you use the pack with shaders, the effect is even better. It looks like the blocks in the game are actually made of paper. But then there's Edge Case Labs, who's doing an even more insane challenge by using paint to recreate all the textures in full color. They're estimating it's going to take 5 years of painting every single day just to complete them all. I downloaded the pack and it was immediately so surreal. It felt like I was playing Minecraft inside an oil painting. They've already completed all the flowers in the game, as well as some of the most iconic textures. Something is so satisfying about seeing all the little imperfections in the pixel art. I especially fell in love with the cactus texture and the amethyst clusters. Doing this amount of artwork for a project is absolutely insane, let alone for a texture pack that's completely free for everyone to download and enjoy. So, do me a big favor and go show these talented creators as much love as you

Every time a new feature gets added to the game, the technical Minecraft community manages to come up with some insane way to use it. But when Mojang announced vibrant visuals, which is basically a built-in shader for the game, I thought that surely this feature would be the exception. Of course, I was wrong. A YouTube channel called Sitback in Minecraft uploaded a video called I made a working sundial using vibrant visuals. Sure enough, this creator managed to calibrate the in-game time to the shadow cast by a post through sheer trial and error. But the original design was pretty big and clunky, so it wasn't necessarily super practical for survival. But all that changed when they had a revelation. It turns out you can throw a trident at the perfect angle, creating a nice skinny shadow with a small footprint. Next, they added in some invisible item frames with a unique item to represent each time of day. And the result is a super cute and fully functional sund dial. I think this is so insanely creative and it's got me wondering what other shadowbased technology might be possible in the

And I'm pretty sure one of my favorite mobs may have just gone completely extinct. And it wouldn't be the first time. In my super flat survival world, we faced three major extinction events in the last decade alone. First, it was the rabbits, which used to spawn in all the usual passive mob biomes. But in 1.9, they sneakily changed it so they only spawn in a few specific biomes. Tragedy struck again in the update aquatic when squids were changed to only spawn in ocean and river biomes, leaving our fountains and caves forever squidless. And finally, in 1.17, it was possible to get the axelottle until they adjusted them in the following update to only spawn in lush caves. But this time, it's glow squid that have gone extinct. And it's all because of one specific bug. This bug changes the sea level in Superflat Worlds to negative 63 instead of positive 63 like it should be, which has all sorts of negative effects. One of which being that phantoms can now spawn at any Y level. Something I mistakenly thought was changed for all worlds until I learned that it's only a super flat problem. But more importantly, the sea level bug makes it impossible to get glow squid in my world. If I had caught this issue in time, it would be no big deal. I could have made a glow squid farm before updating and farmed up as many glow ink sacks as I possibly could. But nobody noticed the issue until I had already upgraded my world, meaning now it's too late. If I want to keep building my cyberpunk end city and filling it with these cool neon signs, then I'm going to have to just pray that this bug gets fixed. Because if it doesn't then

Most people think you can't build below bedrock, but I built an entire ocean biome below the bedrock layer in my world. That's because when Mojang lowered the world height, they never changed the bedrock in Superflat World. So, in 2023, I spent several months breaking over 13,000 blocks of bedrock in my super flat world in order to make room for this underwater ravine. Now, 2 years later, I'd like to expand this ocean all the way to the other side of my base in order to reach my newest mega project, the Vicenium Bay. But, I've mapped out the path that I want the ocean to take. And after some rough calculations, I determined that I would likely have to break close to a million blocks of bedrock. Now, I could just flood the bedrock with water, but that'll just end up looking like a shallow pool and not an ocean. So, instead, I'm going to actually put my art to great use and paint an entire ocean floor pixel art texture. By using different blocks to force shadows and highlights, you can actually create an illusion of depth. Once you add a few layers of water on top, suddenly you have yourself an ocean floor. Do you guys think this looks convincing enough?

I might just be the only person in the world who mines dirt blocks for a living. Let me explain. On April 27th, 2023, I challenged the internet and lost hard. In order to motivate myself to finish a big terraforming project, I promised to mine 50 blocks of dirt for every subscriber I got on the shore. Somehow, I ended up gaining 1 million subscribers on that shore, dooming me to mine 50 million blocks of dirt. Now, most people would have simply given up, but I refused to back down from the impossible challenge I made for myself. Every dirt block must be mined by me, not with TNT. And grass blocks don't count towards the total. I'm doing this 100% legit. And ironically, I've been too busy mining the dirt to even work on the terraforming project that started all of this. After almost 3 years of clearing out all the dirt in my super flat world, I finally officially hit 5 million dirt mine. Just 10% of the WAY DONE. 5 MILLION DIRT. WE GOT IT. WE DID IT, DUDE. APPARENTLY, I've already got the second most dirt mind in the world. And by the time this challenge is over, I'll have the world record by a long shot.

This Minecraft base has one of the coolest reveals I have ever seen. At first, I thought it was just a normal base like any other I visit. My public super flat survival server is over 5 years old now, so people ask me to tour their bases all the time. But when I teleported to the player, Deu Soldier, I was honestly pretty impressed with their interior design skills. They had all the essentials like storage and smelting, plus some flashy stuff, like this armor display. And I'm not even sure how they made this fake spawner, to be honest. But things only kept getting better as I explored the tropical balcony and whatever this weird cat meeting room is. But even as I reached the top floor to see the penthouse suite, I still hadn't realized what was happening. Only when I jumped down and backed way up did it finally become clear. This entire time I had been inside of one of my favorite albums of all time. The first album I ever bought on vinyl. 2010's Plastic Beach by the Gorillas. No way, dude. This is so cool. Plenty of people on the server have made map art of their favorite album, but Deu Soldier brought the album to life and did it all in super flat.

I think I may have accidentally ruined one of the most useful farms in all of Minecraft for everyone. It all started when I made this short about a really old bug in the game that caused the wrong reinforcements to spawn when an undead mob is attacked. For example, when zombified piglins are attacked, they should spawn in zombified piglin reinforcements. But instead, all reinforcements were being spawned as regular zombies. This meant that there was no way to get a trident and super flap because drowned only naturally spawn in aquatic biomes. And the only other way to get a natural drown to spawn is using this reinforcement mechanic. After I called attention to the bug, it ended up finally getting fixed. And now you can get Trident in structuralist superflat or really almost any challenge world that rains and has zombie spawn. But it came at a terrible price because it destroyed copper farms that relied on this bug to function. Before the bug was fixed and drown still spawned in zombie reinforcements, the zombies would take way longer to convert to drowned and then eventually die from the player sweeping edge. Now the drown die out faster than they can be replaced, which means the best source of copper in Super Flat is gone. Of course, this is even more devastating and structural is super flat where you can't get copper from trial chambers. Truly, the Trident has been my monkeykey's paw. So, I'd like to apologize to the entire technical Minecraft community and to everybody watching. Be careful what you wish

I think I made my crappiest build ever. No, literally. I built a bunch of rocks covered in bird poop in my forever world. And although they may look innocent, they have some weirdly deep lore. I was originally inspired by the sea stacks I saw while visiting the cliffs of Moore in Ireland and in some photos I've seen of the Oregon coastline, which I'm hoping to visit soon. I thought the bird poop stains actually added a lot of atmosphere and character to these rocks. And I was really happy with how it translated into the game. But as I continued looking for reference photos of bird crap, I ended up flushing myself down a crazy rabbit hole. This sort of fossilized bird poop is known as guano. And since it's full of plant goodies like nitrogen, phosphate, and potassium, it makes insanely good fertilizer. Back in the 1800s, the British controlled most of the guano trade. So the 13th US President, Millard Filillmore, made bird poop a top priority. Congress passed the Guano Islands Act of 1856, which basically said that any American who found a rock with guano on it in the ocean could essentially just declare it US territory. Eventually, synthetic fertilizer was invented, but in the meantime, almost 100 different tiny islands got claimed. And somehow, some of them still exist as US territory to this day. So, basically, bird poop imperialism was and is still a thing. And the people who mine this stuff suffered from all sorts of crazy respiratory illnesses and horrible work conditions. Thank goodness we don't have anything like that in Minecraft.

Minecraft players can take the most ordinary block and somehow find a way to make it functional. But when Mojang added vibrant visuals to the game, which is basically a built-in shader pack, I thought finally there was a feature that was purely about aesthetics. Of course, this YouTuber sitback in Minecraft immediately proved me wrong by making this beautiful and functional sundial that uses the shadow cast by a trident to tell time. So, I started wondering if any other shadow-based technology might be possible. And sure enough, just a few days ago, Sitback and Minecraft followed up with another video promising a door unlocked with vibrant visuals. Some of you guys had the same idea in the comments, but I figured it would be impossible since the shadows and light don't actually affect daylight sensors or get detected by observers. But the actual solution is so creative. The door uses a redstone combination lock that gives the user an array of many buttons. At a specific time of day, the sun shines through holes in the slope roof and lights up the buttons you need to press to open the door. Another more refined version of the puzzle uses the Trident Sun dial to show the user what time of day the key to the door is revealed. At just the right time, you're able to see the three keywords light up on the cave wall. Then you can rename paper with the correct keywords and throw it onto the floor to unlock the hidden entrance.

Hi. I think Minecraft may have recently made one of the worst changes in the history of the game, and I haven't seen a single person talk about it. For as long as I can remember, my super flat survival world has been pretty peaceful at night. But then recently, I upgraded my world to version 1.21.7. At first, nothing seemed that different. Some minor tweaks to the fog, but not much else. But then, when I forgot to sleep for a few nights, it happened. Ever since they were added to the game, phantoms only ever spawned when the player was above Y 64. And since my super flat world's bedrock layer is at Y level zero, it was honestly pretty rare that I ever went high enough for phantoms to spawn. But apparently in 1.21.5, with almost nobody noticing, Mojang quietly changed the phantom spawning requirements to only need a direct line of sight to the sky. In other words, the player's Y level no longer matters. So, for the first time in 13 years, my world has been completely overrun with phantoms, and it's a nightmare. I finally see why everyone is constantly complaining about these things. And it's not just a super flat problem, either. If you're in a cave with any sky access, they can get you there, too. I thought maybe bringing a cat everywhere with me would help, but the truth is that the most annoying thing about them isn't them swooping down to attack. It's the terrible banshee shrieks they make all night long. I'm not sure why this was even changed, but I hope they change it back because I don't know about you, but I only want to see phantoms when

My entire YouTube channel is a lie. For years now, I've been the guy who loves the wandering trader. I've spent so much time telling people about how important he is to my super flat world and all the blocks and items I couldn't get without him. I nicknamed him Wandi T. I built him an entire mansion. And I even made a video begging people to stop killing him all the time, which got over 13 million views. But now I must make 13 million apologies. And it's all because I'm on a mission to get the blue axelottle in Superflat. For that, I need buckets of tropical fish, which I can only get from the wandering trader. It's such a rare trade, and I need so many fish buckets that I've been afking all day and night at my trader farm. And when I'm done trading, the traders need to be disposed of to prevent them from building up and entity cramming, which has all led to possibly the worst stat in my entire super flat world. I'm so ashamed. But Wy T is turning 7 years old on January 30th. So, to make up for my sins, I'm going to make him the biggest birthday cake in Minecraft history. For every single subscriber that this short gets, I'm going to add another cake onto the mega cake. But guys, cakes don't even stack.

I went inside the Mojang Studio in Stockholm, Sweden, and I saw new features there that could change the future of Minecraft forever. It all began when I got an email inviting me out to visit the Mojang HQ. But first, I had to find my way there, which was going to be tough because I've never been to Sweden. I flew for basically an entire day before finally reaching the land of the midnight sun. When I got to the airport, I knew I must be getting close. Everything felt so Scandinavian, from my hotel room to the local cuisine. But just as the old buildings and carless streets began to melt my feeble American mind, I finally found the office. Inside was the greatest collection of Minecraft stuff I have ever seen in my life. I honestly felt like the king of the world. But then I started running into some familiar faces, and we noticed a weird amount of camera equipment everywhere. That's when Mojang employee grabbed us and began showing us all the new features that are coming to the game. And now I get the chance to personally help show you. I'm going to be in the Minecraft Live After show on September 27th starting at 9:30 a.m. Pacific time.

Since gold in Minecraft has no good variations like stairs or slabs for building, I often find myself using other blocks like bamboo to represent gold. Sometimes I mix in horn coral blocks for some roughness or yellow glazed terracotta to get that sort of chiseled look. But what I find really funny is that all of those blocks are actually more valuable than gold itself in my super flat world. Gold is one of the only ore blocks I can get in super flat because I have access to the Nether. And you can easily make AFKable gold farms to get huge amounts. Meanwhile, bamboo was impossible to get at all until trial chambers added bamboo planks to some loot chests. But it's still non-renewable because I can't get the actual bamboo plant. So, I have to do tons of dungeon crawling to get it. Even harder is the coral, which I can only buy from Wani T whenever he decides to sell it. He only shows up every 4 and 1 half hours of play time on average, and he has a ton of different trades, so getting horn coral specifically is very rare, and you can only buy a few blocks at a time. And then there's the glazed terracotta, which has to be traded for. That means you need to roll for a mason villager that sells the exact block you want out of 32 glazed and non-glazed options. And to even unlock the terracotta trade, each villager needs to be leveled up to expert. So, prepare to waste a lot of emeralds. That's if you don't want to go through the process of converting dirt into mud, into clay, into terracotta, and then dying it yellow. I made a farm to do all that converting for me and I built that as well as all the cool stuff you saw in this short in the related

After 13 years in my Minecraft world, I'm still finding new ways to improve in the game all the time. But I never thought Disneyland would be where I found my new favorite technique for making my world feel immersive. I went to the park last spring and I loved how enveloping the theming felt. So, I wanted to learn more about how they pull it off. That's when I found this video by George Browning talking all about sight lines. Sight lines are a consideration in the design of urban planning and architecture with super varied applications. It can be a safety concern like making sure drivers can see other cars coming or it can be more about value like making sure that the people in a theater can get a good view of the show from any seat so you can sell more tickets. Disney Imagineers use sightelines all the time to block out oftheme areas from view within their parks. For example, George Browning shows off this triangular shaped tree that perfectly blocks the Matterhorn mountain ride from view since seeing the Swiss Alps would totally ruin the feel of the New Orleans Square. In my latest super flat build in the related video, I wanted to make an ancient temple that feels super hidden, like a secret sanctuary. So, by carefully placing a ruined church and a dozen or so custom trees to block the sight lines, I was able to perfectly obscure the area from most angles. This forces the player to actually enter the area on foot, where the trees frame a perfect reveal of the ancient statue inside. I guess I have no choice but to go to Disney more often. Thanks, Disneyland.

A player logs onto a survival server full of ambition, ready to start grinding for a new mega project. But as they enter their first N city ship, their heart sinks. The elytra has already been looted. No big deal. The search continues until they check another and another. Another another. Some evil nerd has already stolen every single elytra on the server and is now selling them for 10 Netherite blocks each in a creepy van at spawn. Sound familiar? The thing is, Moang already fixed this issue in 1.21. Kind of. When trial chambers were added to the game, they added vaults which can be opened using a trial key. Each vault can be looted once per player, and the keys can be farmed using the trial spawner. It's a great system to allow everyone a chance to loot a structure, except that they weren't added to any of the game's old structures. If bench ships had a few vaults with a chance to get elytra, people wouldn't have to explore thousands of blocks for key progression items. But 1. Not 21 also added the ominous potion, which activates pillager raids and corresponds to a special ominous vault in the trial chamber. It's such a good opportunity to take old structures and add brand new challenges and rewards to them. Like, what if the ominous effect at a bastion activates some sort of piglin seed? or a chicken jockey fight at the woodland mansion.

This is the Birch Creek, a super rare mob that spawns when a player hasn't slept in at least 100 days and places a creaking heart in a birch forest. The only issue with this mob is that I completely made it up for April Fool's Day. But so many people wanted to see this added to the game that I made a data pack to change any creaking spawn in a birch forest to get the cool texture I made. Since it's a client side data pack, it even works on servers. But there were even more commenters who wanted to see more types of logs added. I started texturing a few of them, but then I was like, "Hold on, someone's probably done this already." And sure enough, I found a mod, and it's way cooler than I even imagined. It's called Grafted Creaking by dialing spoon 527. And not only does it have all the wood types and a really cool mushroom variant to live amongst the giant shrooms, but it even allows you to mix and match by placing different logs above and below the creaking heart. You can spawn in some crazy variations. And each creaking has unique abilities. Like the bamboo one multiplies when attacked, and the mangrove one can walk on water. The mushroom one actually attacks hostile mobs, which makes so much sense because hostile mobs can't spawn in mushroom island biomes. I'd love to see someone out there use the mushroom creaking to design an insane hostile mob farm in survival.

There's one crucial thing you can do to completely upgrade your build. Stop planting trees and start building trees. Having custom trees in your world is one of the easiest ways to bring life to your projects. A great example is this cliff side I built in the related video, which literally took me over 23 hours of solid gameplay. I'm proud of how it came out, but without vegetation, it still feels lifeless. But as soon as I started adding trees, the area literally came to life. Just looking at the before and after makes the difference super apparent. When it comes to building trees, I first focus on establishing a great branch structure by using a combination of various spruce components and mud bricks and glass paint. The glass works almost like a debug stick, allowing you to connect the walls together to form smooth curves in the trunk and limbs. From there, the key is to gradient the leaves with the darker leaves on the bottom and lighter leaves on top where the sun would hit. You also got to make sure not to overdo it with the leaves, especially on the bottom. When you stand below a tree in real life, it's easy to see the branch structure and all the patches of sunlight peeking through. Only from above do they appear fluffy and full. I have a rule in my world that every single tree must be completely unique as in nature. No copy pasting and rotating the same tree all over the place.

It's always bugged me that cake is pretty much the only food item in Minecraft that you can place. I mean, what makes cake so different from pumpkin pie? And then when I got to go to the Minecraft movie premiere, they had these cubic fruit and vegetable props. But a lot of you in the comments pointed out that in the game, they're round. But I just saw a video that solves this entire issue. It's a resource pack called Fresh Food by Siberia, and it has these stunning eating animations to turn every food item into an actual 3D object. When I put the pack on, I quickly ran around my flat world eating as many food items as I could possibly find. I think the suspicious stew is my favorite. But then I noticed that Siberia has another pack called Fresh Buckets that makes the buckets appear as 3D objects as well. The axelottle is so cute. And then I tried out fresh music discs which also looked pretty cool. I thought that was it until I joined Siberia's Discord and I realized that there was a brand new pack in beta. Fresh flowers and plants. So I went to my greenhouse to try holding some plants with the brand new pack and everything was transformed. The mushrooms look so cool like this. And I love the basket of flower petals. Apparently Siberia is slowly working on making 3D versions of all the items in the game. His next project is creating unique book textures for every single enchantment.

Minecraft just revealed a brand new mob. And at first, I thought it wouldn't be possible to get in my world until I looked deeper. The Nautilus is basically a new underwater mount that pauses your breath meter, allowing you to easily travel and build underwater. These would be so perfect for my forever world because I have this huge underwater base called Salatia that I've been wanting to upgrade. And since I play in a super flat world, there's no way to get the buried treasure needed to build a conduit. But unfortunately, since Nautilus only spawned in ocean biomes, I was pretty certain I would be unable to get the mob at all due to my super flat world being entirely one biome planes. But then I remembered the reinforcement mechanic. Hitting an undead mob causes it to summon more undead mobs of the same type. Meaning that in theory I could gather a bunch of drowned and keep punching them until I got a rare Nautilus jockey spawn. The issue is that nautilus jockeyies are technically a whole different mob, the zombie nautilus. And as far as I know, there's no way to cure a zombie nautilus into a normal one. But maybe this is the time for Mojang to add that feature. If you could cure a zombie horse into a horse with golden carrots, it would give players another option for obtaining horses. Curing a zombie nautilus into a nautilus would make them obtainable in most single biome challenge worlds. And you could even add the ability to cure a zombie piglin into a normal piglin, allowing for pigment bartering. This would make it possible to go to the Nether and get stone for the first time in structuralist challenge worlds. So let it be known we must find a cure for the zombie pandemic.

I just completed my most expensive project ever in my 13-year forever world. But you'd never know it just by looking at it. I mean, sure, there are Netherite blocks in the eyes of the statue, but other than that, it seems like a fairly normal build. But because my world is actually a super flat world, the things that are rare for me are completely different from what would be rare in a normal world. For example, all the deep slate and tough blocks hidden in the terraforming would be super common blocks in a normal world. But I can only get tough from the chests and trial chambers. And deep slate is only found within one room of the trial chambers, which gives you a pathetic 12 blocks. But I'm only just getting started. The whole point of the build is to help me on my quest for the blue axelottle that I went on in the related video. So I rigged up a big fireworks show to go off once I reach my goal. But in order to get the fancy trail effect on the fireworks stars, I use 35 diamonds, which I can only get from loot chests. But probably the most expensive item in all of Superflat is the amethyst block, which is crafted with four amethyst shards. And you can only get those from this one specific loot chest in the trial chambers. I wanted my temple to feel magical, so I hid 18 of these blocks under moss just so you get that cool twinkle sound when you walk over them. But if all that weren't enough, then I started making donations to the axelottle gods in this sacrificial fountain to try and bless my RNG. I spent 10 more diamonds, all of the netherite I had, a nether star, a stack of deep slate, and an entire stack of amethyst shards. Oh, that made me feel nauseous to say out loud. And that kids is why you never play the Axel

This is one of the most valuable chests in my 13-year-old super flat survival world, my ink sack stash. I collected these back when squids could spawn in any biome. I had some nether portals with a water elevator that I made to get up and down, but it ended up spawning squids and instantly sending them to the Nether. But then in 2018, they made it so squid could only spawn in rivers and oceans, making them extinct in my world. Now, the only way to get ink sacks is by fishing them up, and it's a one in a,000 chance to get 10 of them. I only ever use these ink sacks for book and quills, which I need a lot of if I'm ever going to fill up my library with readable books. So, every time I need black dye, I have to go on a chicken murder rampage in order to get wither flowers. It takes ages, and their desperate clucks echo throughout my nightmares. But in the end, it's all worth it

The Minecraft Copper Age drop is finally here, but there's one tricky issue preventing me from upgrading my Forever World to get the new content, and it probably affects your world, too. The drop adds copper variants of tons of blocks in the game, like chests, chains, and iron bars, which are all amazing for decorating. But it also adds oxidation stages to lightning rods, meaning that every single lightning rod I've ever placed in my world will begin changing colors if I upgrade. And obviously, I can't wax them before upgrading because that wasn't a thing before. So, I'll somehow have to go around and locate every single rod I've placed and wax them as soon as possible after upgrading, which is honestly a small problem for me, but these lightning rods have been so useful for pipes and railings that I know some players who have literally placed tens of thousands of these in their survival worlds, and they're all about to turn blue. To be nice to the community, Mojang could pre-wax all of our rods for us when we update to the new drop. But instead, I have a feeling this will go down as the update where all of our rods blew up.

For about 12 years, it was completely impossible for me to get snow in my super flat world. But the worst part wasn't being able to decorate with it or not being able to use snow golems in my farms. It was all the people constantly telling me I just needed to build high up in the world and wait for snow, which is not how it works in Planes Biomes, and it never has. I mean, it does snow if you go up high enough, but that level is way above height limit. But all this finally changed when Mojang added in the trial chambers. I was able to go into one of the chambers and grab both powder snow and enough snowballs to make a snow golem and get an infinite supply. But even though I'd been wanting snow for over a decade, I just kind of put it in a Shulker box and moved on. It's already been like a year and a half and I just never really did anything with it. So since it's Christmas, I decided to finally remove the white carpet covering my cobblestone mountain that's been up there forever and instead placed some beautiful snow layers. Then I put up a Christmas tree complete with fancy ornaments and a nether star at the top. I made sure to make some presents for all my best friends like Mountain Pig, Wy Tea, and all of my cats. And I even made a present for you guys, the viewers. But all I want for Christmas is for you to subscribe.

This Minecraft block just sent me down the weirdest rabbit hole. It's called glow lykan, and I haven't thought about it much since it was added to the game, mostly because I'm not able to get it in my super flat world. But some builders have actually used it for some really cool techniques, like putting it on top of glazed terracotta to achieve a nice stained glass look. I always thought Lyan in real life was just a fungus, but I decided to look into it, and it's actually way weirder than that. Lykan is actually a sort of team up between fungus and algae. The fungus pays the rent, providing a cozy home for the algae, and the algae puts food on the table by utilizing photosynthesis. The two were such a power couple that they basically invented the term symbiosis in biology, which is a mutually beneficial relationship between two organisms of different species. Albert Bernard Frank was the first to use the term when he described the mutualistic relationship found in Lyken back in 1877, which was a few years before glow lyken was introduced into Minecraft. Lyken doesn't actually glow at all in real life, but there are bioluminescent fungi out there like fox fire, which can be found on rotting wood and gives off a dim bluish green glow on dark nights. It would definitely be cool to see bioluminescent forests in the game. If you learn something new, make sure to glow, like, and subscribe.

For years now, the wandering trader has been the punching bag of the Minecraft community, and it drives me crazy. I started playing my super flat survival world in 2012, and for the seven years until he was added, the only wood type I had was oak. I didn't have access to basic crafting items like sand and sugarcane, and even entire colors were impossible to get without his cactus and die trade. And with recent updates, the list of items I rely on him for has expanded dramatically. Basically, everything changed when WanT was introduced, and he's probably the only reason I'm still playing in my world today. So, I've been telling people to stop killing him for a long time, but the excuse is always the same. I did it for the leads. Well, that's finally about to change. Mojang adjusted the crafting recipe for leads in the upcoming drop to no longer require slime balls, making them so much easier to craft early game. Not only that, but they added the ability to sever leads with shears, which allows you to take the wandering trader leads easily without having to kill him or his llamas. At this point, there is no excuse anymore. From now on, if you try and touch my boy, you're going to have to go through

All right, so the final big snapshot for the happy gas drop just hit and we can now craft saddles and remove saddles from mobs with shears. But we also got five new ambient tracks in the game, plus a new music disc called Tears that you get when you kill a gas with a deflected fireball. Plus, we can now toggle music to play constantly and we have the option to turn on this little display that says what song is playing. But how good are the new songs? So all the original music for Minecraft is by C418, and I personally think it's the best and most Minecraft sounding music in the game. But even though he wrote an entire new album for Minecraft, rumors are that Microsoft won't use it because C418 wants to retain the copyrights to his own music. So, in the past several years, we've seen other composers add to the game soundtrack, like Lena Rain, who worked on Celeste, and Kumi Tanoka, who worked on Final Fantasy 11. Lena Ran also collabed with Aaron Sherof on the Tricky Trial soundtrack, which makes sense because both are based in Seattle, where the American Mojang office is located. But this drop uses brand new composer named Amos Rody based in Portland, Oregon. Oh, this new music disc has like a nightclub sound to it. Very ghostly. It definitely fits in with Pixstep. All right, this one is for frozen peaks biomes. Yeah, a lot of these are pretty ambient, very soft, and peaceful. I honestly think these might be the closest to that classic C418 vibe. Dude, this one sounds so much like the old Minecraft music. I give this a solid 8 out of 10. Very Minecraft.

I'm about to remove this sign, which will cause all 60,000 of these cakes to instantly explode, and possibly my PC as well. But why did I even make this monstrosity in the first place? It was all to celebrate the Wandering Trader, who just turned 7 years old a few weeks ago. He provides my super flat world with so many different blocks and items I could never get in the past. So, for his birthday, I wanted to make him the world record biggest cake in Minecraft. I challenged myself to place a cake for every subscriber I got in the related short, and a whopping 60,000 of you subscribed. But placing all the cakes was a huge struggle cuz you can't actually stack cakes in Java Edition. I ended up getting 28 farmer villagers who sell cakes, which took forever since it's only a 29% chance that the farmer gets the trade. Plus, the trade unlocks at the expert level, so I had to level up so many villagers. I traded so many carrots and potatoes that I had to spend hours building up brand new auto carrot and potato farms cuz my old ones just couldn't keep up. It took me dozens of hours of placing cakes to get this done. And I even lost my mind along the way. The cakes were so laggy that my game literally started running slower, making it even harder to keep placing them. But with the world record finally secured, it was time to break the sign and watch our masterpiece explode. In 3 2 1 Oh, my PC.

These six vanilla plus mods enhance the way Minecraft looks without changing any gameplay. When Mojang added the falling leaves in the spring drop update, I knew I needed more immersive animations like that in the game. So, here's what I found. The explosive enhancement mod by Supercat 32 adds this gorgeous animation to explosions, and it even has an underwater variant. The wakes mod by GOI56 adds splashes and wakes into the game, and they look so Minecrafty. It pairs very nicely with some of the features from the particular mod by Chai, which adds 3D water splashes and waterfall cascades, plus some other neat stuff like cave dust and bubbles from underwater chests. The particle rain mod by Pig Cart really improves all the weather in the game. Rain is much less cluttered and falls at an angle as if blown by the wind. Snowflakes also look much better and they rotate gently as they fall. It also adds these amazing dust storms in desert biomes, water ripples on water surfaces during the rain, and these water droplets on certain walls. There's also this really unique looking ground fog that appears in humid biomes. If you pair that with the cool rain mod by Jay's, which creates these super immersive sounds for rain based on what material is falling on, rain quickly goes from a nuisance to a cozy vibe. And last, but not least, there's visuality by Pink Goose, which adds sparkles to armor and ores, gooey slime particles when slimes hop around, and bones when you strike skeletons. Hopefully I just helped make your two week Minecraft phase way more vibe.

Last fall, during the leadup to the copper age drop, Mojang released a mysterious short showing all the new copper features in a row. But the camera kept panning to reveal two dandelions placed perfectly side by side, just like they would be on the back of a Mulum. This short seemed like such an intentional teaser that it sent me into full conspiracy mode, and I was absolutely convinced that the Mloom was going to be added to the drop. But the copper age came and went, and our beloved piss cow was nowhere to be found. But I think I might know why. In the next drop, Mojang is updating the design of every single baby mob in the game. This is something their art team would handle. And since there are so many baby mobs to update, it's likely that they've been working on this behind the scenes for months now. This is even backed up by the baby Nautilus having similar one pixel eyes to all of the new baby mobs. We also know that when Mojang adds old mob concepts, they typically change their function. When the copper golem was originally proposed in the mob vote, he would randomly press buttons and switches, but they eventually changed it so his primary function was sorting chests. When the Mloom was first proposed, we were only given a vague hint that it would somehow interact with bees. But what if the plan for the Golden Dandelion was already devised last fall? The Golden Dandelion is a new item in the game that prevents baby mobs from growing into adults when fed to them. This item feels perfect for the Mubloom. So, could it be possible that they ended up delaying the addition of the Mubloom to fit in better with this baby mob drop? Or is this a sign that the Mubloom will stay sheld and forgotten forever? Personally, I'm going to keep believing.

What started out as a wholesome beach day on my server, quickly went completely off the rails. It all started when one of the members, Amy Bunny, had the idea to hold a summer event. We started with the fishing tourney, which lasted 1 hour, and somehow my team actually won. We actually >> I promise it wasn't rigged. Then we moved on to the high dive competition. This is my Patreon only structuralist super flat server, so we're really limited on pallet and we have to use stuff like wool for stone. I actually did pretty well in the high dive, too. But in the end, I couldn't compete with the best of the server. Oh, and he made it. People even did some diving tricks, which I've never seen before. My favorite event by far was the sand castle competition. Some people went all out, creating lore for their builds and even filling all the chests with loot. But it all fell apart when we got to the summer skin competition. I brought my classic vintage swim trunks, and I actually made it into the top three. But others on the server got totally carried away. >> Oh no. >> Oh no. And that's how

People keep asking me how and why I have so many petrified oak slabs in my super flat world. It all started 13 years ago when I first decided to try out super flat survival. The first thing I did was run to the closest village and begin looting all the resources there. Then once I had some basic stone tools, I began digging a giant circle in the ground which would eventually become my main base to this day. But when I reached the bedrock layer, I encountered a big problem. Mobs were spawning everywhere. And since at the time you needed a light level of seven to prevent them, I would have had to spam torches all over the place to fix it. So instead, I covered the bedrock with wooden slabs to prevent the mob spawns. But right around that time, Mojang decided to expand the world height from 128 to 256. This meant that for the first time, you could actually build on the roof of the Nether. And since it was completely dark up there, mobs would spawn absolutely everywhere, and you would barely get any mobs below the Nether roof. Now, Mojang could have just made it so that mobs can't spawn above the roof of the Nether. But instead, they did something much cooler. They made it so that mobs can't spawn on bedrock instead, meaning that you could still build mob farms on the Nether roof out of different blocks. And [music] more importantly, it meant that I no longer needed my half slab floor. So, I ripped it all out and put the slabs into these chests for safekeeping. They're fireproof and are broken with a pickaxe instead of an axe, so they're pretty cursed. Eventually, Mojang changed the way that wooden slabs work in the game, and these slabs became petrified oak slabs. For a long time, they didn't even have a proper texture. Do you guys have any idea what I should do with these

The mood might just be coming to Minecraft. Everyone's heard by now that the copper golem's coming to the game in the next drop along with copper chests, armor, and tools. But the copper golem actually lost the mob vote to the LA back in 2021. It was a tight race between the two from the beginning since the glare's description wasn't quite as impressive as the other two. So, this begs the question, could more former mob vote candidates be making their way into the game? The copper golem actually wasn't the first loser to be added. The swap biome features were added in the 1.19 update after originally losing the biome vote to the mountain. This included the frogs, meaning these were really the first loser mob to be added. But Mojang appears to be teasing even more. The short that they uploaded to the Minecraft channel to announce the copper golem seems to have a sneak peek at the very end, which I noticed thanks to some of the comments. Two dandelions are in a perfect line with the other features in the last shot of the video. Almost like the mushrooms you see on the backs of mushrooms. The isoler was actually the second place winner in the mob vote that year, which the glow squid won. So maybe this isn't enough evidence for you, but personally I

Whenever I play Minecraft, I always end up creating an entire religion. I don't mean to do it. It It just kind of happens. Like over a decade ago, I built this random spider farm because I needed a bunch of string for the trip wires in my mob farm. But since everyone just uses cave spider spawners for string farms, and at the time, mine shafts didn't generate in super flat, I had to come up with my own design for a spider only mob farm. It ended up floating next to my base for years until I finally decided to cover it up with something. And since I'd never built a cathedral before, I decided to take a stab at it. It only made sense to start adding spiderlike features to the build. and before I knew it, my church had become a spir. One thing led to another, and before long, I found myself building a giant statue of the almighty spider god on top of my base. Or there was that time back in high school when after playing Bioshock, I wanted to try building an underwater base. I thought a statue would look nice in front of the structure, but before I knew it, I was writing pages of lore about the great war between the spider god and the protectors of the sea. But my latest religion might be the most insane of all. I wanted to get the blue axelottle in my super flat world, which is pretty close to impossible to pull off. So, naturally, I built an entire redstone powered temple in the related video, complete with sacrificial rituals, Zelda style murals, and mysterious note block hymns. I have to take off my elytra just to enter these sacred grounds, and I routinely pray to a giant bucket called the great vessel. So I guess the only question is which religion are you joining?

Some of you might think of the newly released Chase the Skies game drop as just a happy gas, but there are tons of little features that I'll bet you miss. Like, did you know they added a brand new advancement called stay hydrated? You get it by placing the dried gas in water. And speaking of the dried gas, did you know it's actually craftable? You can also craft a new harness for the guests. And even more surprisingly, you can now finally craft saddles. You might have heard this banger new music disc that you can get from hitting a gas with its own fireball. But there are also five other ambient tracks that play in different biomes. My favorite is below and above, which gives me such nostalgic beta Minecraft vibes. There's been a lot of hype about vibrant visuals, which is a new graphic setting that makes Minecraft look stunning. But did you know that as part of this upgrade, the entire fog system in the game was enhanced? It's hard to believe how atmospheric the game looks now. There are even more small changes like leads no longer needing slime balls, shears being able to cut leads and remove saddles, and carrots now being able to be used for taming or breeding donkeys, mules, and horses. Not to mention UI improvements like the locator bar and the ability to toggle how the music plays. Be honest. Did any of these changes sneak by

I don't think anyone really understands how impossible it is to get the blue axelottle in Superflat. I mean, sure, it took my friend Womba around 40 hours to get one in his world, which is pretty brutal. But according to my calculations, in Superflat, it's not going to take hours. It's going to take years. See, normally you just go to a warm ocean, scoop up some tropical fish, and start breeding. But in Superflat, the only way to get live tropical fish is this guy. The watering trader always has two purchasing trades, two special trades, and five ordinary trades. And the tropical fish bucket is just one out of 73 possible ordinary trades. This means every time he shows up, which is about every 4.76 hours of gameplay, you've got a measly 6.8% chance of getting the trade. So on average, you have to play for 70 hours just to see the trade once. And since he only sells enough fish for two breeding attempts, you need him to show up with the bucket 600 times to hit the 1 and 1200 spawn. That is 4.79 years of non-stop uninterrupted gameplay before you're statistically expected to see a blue axelotta. To really put that in perspective, imagine you're spending money at a rate of $7 per second. If you started with $1 million, you'd go broke in about 40 hours, roughly the time it took Womba to get his axelott. But if you started with $1 billion, it would take [music] you $4 years to spend it on. So getting the blue axelottle in Superflat isn't just harder. It's literally the difference between your average moneysavvy baby boomer and Taylor

This might look like a bowl of craft, but it's actually so much more valuable than you think. That's because brown dye is the only non-renewable color in Super Flat since there's no way to obtain cocoa beans and no way to craft it from other dyes. That means that literally the only way I can get brown dye is by buying it from the wandering trader. The only catch is that the trade is pretty rare, only appearing about 7% of the time. People ask me all the time why I haven't built a swamp in my world since my name is, you know, Mog Swamp. And the real answer is because I've been waiting for enough brown dye. I'm obsessed with this one build trick where you put a brown candle on top of green glass panes to create cattails. And when I finally build a swamp, I want to absolutely fill it with these. But so far, I've only collected about 7 and 12 stacks of brown dye. Which means I still can't even make 500 of these cattails. And that's just not going to cut it for my swamp. Which is why I live by the motto, when in doubt, ABBD. Always buy brown dye

Do you guys remember that old video of the Star Trek ship built in Minecraft from like 15 years ago? For me, it's one of the most nostalgic Minecraft videos on YouTube. The creator, Halcun, shows off a 1:1 scale model of the USS Enterprise D, which was insanely impressive for the time. I mean, back then, you couldn't even fly around in creative, so you had to use scaffolding to get around the build. But not many people know that this video launched a whole server called Trecraft, where dedicated treers have spent the last 15 years perfecting these ships. And when I say perfecting, I really mean it. I was able to hop on the server and when I set foot on the Enterprise, I was truly blown away. The interior was fully finished and even the pipes and wires between the floors were accurate. I mean, you could literally spend hours and hours exploring this ship without seeing everything. The scale is truly impossible to communicate through video. But luckily, you can actually hop on the server to see the incredible ships yourself. Or at least you could. The server just shut down on New Year's Eve. The current admin needed a break to focus on their health. and they expressed that until a new caretaker can be identified, the server will be offline [music] indefinitely. Who knows, maybe someone watching this short will have what it takes to continue this incredible server's legacy. But if not, I think it's important that we all take a moment to appreciate this server's accomplishments and its legendary place in Minecraft

Usually, I love making Minecraft shorts, but I'd give anything not to have to make this one. It all began a year ago when I joined a no armor hardcore server called Naked and Afraid with a few other content creators. Pathetically, I only managed to last 6 minutes before I was obliterated by a pillager. I used to be the super flat guy, but ever since then, I'm just the guy who died first in Naked and Afraid. Everywhere I go on the internet, people seem to taunt me for it. But finally, I had a chance to redeem myself because a month or two ago, the gang announced it was time for Naked and Afraid season 2. I've actually gotten a lot better at the game since last year, and this time there's 20 people, so my only goal was to not be the first one out again. That being said, this year it's ultra hardcore, so there's no natural health regeneration, which makes things a lot harder. Now, this is normally the part of the short where I tell you to go watch the related video to find out what happened. But honestly, all you have to do is look at the posters that got made, and I think you can figure it out. Everyone on the server is supposed to advertise these posters, but to tell you the truth, I don't want any of you to have this image on your wall at home. In fact, maybe we could just forget I was ever on the server. Can I please just be the super flat guy
